required.title: [Read] The Story of the Jews, Volume 1
required.author: Simon Schama (selected chapters)

required.title: [Read] Christianity: The First Three Thousand Years
required.author: Diarmaid MacCulloch (selected chapters)

competence_markers.description: The student can map Judaism as a worldview using the six diagnostic questions
The student can map Christianity as a worldview using the six diagnostic questions
The student can explain the covenant, the Torah, and the Talmudic tradition with specific detail
The student can explain the Incarnation, the Trinity, and salvation theology with specific detail
The student can identify the major Christian denominations and their key differences
The student can present the honest record of both traditions — achievements and failures
The student can steel-man the tradition furthest from their own experience
The student understands the relationship between Judaism and Christianity, including the painful history

narration_prompts.detail: What is it? Who is it between? What does each side promise?
What does it claim? Why is it the most important claim in the tradition?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the Talmud, and why is it remarkable?
narration_prompts.detail: What does it tell you about what Judaism values?

narration_prompts.prompt: Map either Judaism or Christianity using the six diagnostic questions.
narration_prompts.detail: Present the worldview as a system.

narration_prompts.prompt: What did Christianity produce that shaped the world?
narration_prompts.detail: Name at least three specific contributions.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell the honest story of one of Christianity's failures
narration_prompts.detail: — the Crusades, the Inquisition, or Christian antisemitism. What happened? How was it justified? What does it teach us?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the relationship between Judaism and Christianity?
narration_prompts.detail: How are they connected? Where did they diverge? What has the relationship cost?

cross_connections.description: The Crusades, Christendom, and the medieval church are covered in depth.
Zoroastrian influence on Jewish theology — Satan, angels, resurrection, apocalyptic thought.
The diagnostic framework applied here was developed there.
The relationship between religious authority and law — Torah law, canon law, natural law theory.

content.why_this_matters:
Judaism and Christianity are the two traditions most responsible for the moral, legal, and institutional architecture of Western civilization. The concept of universal human rights, the structure of Western law, the university system, the hospital system, the abolition of slavery, the calendar you use — all trace back to ideas that originated in these two traditions.

They are also locked in one of history's most complex and painful relationships. Christianity grew directly out of Judaism — Jesus was a Jew, the apostles were Jews, the earliest Christians were Jews. And yet, for nearly two thousand years, Christians persecuted Jews — sometimes viciously — in the name of the Christian God. This relationship must be understood honestly, without softening either the extraordinary contributions or the extraordinary harm.

This packet presents Judaism and Christianity from the inside — as their most thoughtful practitioners understand them — before engaging in any critique. The goal is a student who can articulate the internal logic of both traditions so accurately that a devout Jew or a devout Christian would recognize the account as fair.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Judaism as a complete worldview** — covenant theology, Torah and Talmud, the prophetic tradition, diaspora resilience
2. **Christianity as a complete worldview** — incarnation, Trinity, salvation, church history, denominational diversity
3. **The relationship between the two** — the shared roots and the painful divergence
4. **The six diagnostic questions applied** — mapping both traditions as worldview systems
5. **The honest record** — both achievements and failures, presented without apology or condemnation
6. **Steel-manning** — presenting each tradition at its strongest
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Judaism — The Covenant as Architecture

Judaism is not merely the religion that preceded Christianity. It is one of history's most extraordinary intellectual and spiritual traditions — a tradition that survived the destruction of its temple, the loss of its homeland, and nearly two millennia of persecution, and emerged not diminished but deepened.

**The covenant (brit):** The central structure of Judaism. God made a covenant with Abraham: I will be your God; you will be my people; I will give you a land; through you all nations will be blessed. This covenant was renewed with Moses at Sinai, where God gave the Torah — the law that defines what it means to be the people of God.

The covenant is bilateral. God has obligations; the people have obligations. It can be violated — and the Hebrew Bible is full of stories of violation and return. But it cannot be broken permanently. Teshuvah — repentance, return — is always possible. This is a moral psychology of extraordinary power: you can always come back.

**The Torah:** The first five books of the Hebrew Bible — Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy. The Torah contains 613 commandments (mitzvot) covering every aspect of life: worship, diet, justice, sexual conduct, agriculture, property, care for the poor. Judaism is a way of life, not just a set of beliefs. To be a Jew is to do Judaism — to follow the law, to observe the Sabbath, to keep kosher, to pray.

**The Talmud:** One of the most remarkable intellectual projects in human history. The Talmud is the record of centuries of rabbinic debate about the meaning and application of the Torah. It does not give definitive answers — it records arguments. Dissenting opinions are preserved alongside majority rulings. The tradition of argument-as-worship — the belief that debating the law is itself a sacred act — is unique to Judaism and has profoundly shaped Jewish intellectual culture.

**The prophets:** Isaiah, Jeremiah, Amos, Hosea, Micah — voices of moral conscience who called the people and their rulers back to covenant faithfulness. The prophets insisted that God cares about justice more than ritual: "What does the Lord require of you? To act justly, to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God" (Micah 6:8). This prophetic tradition — the demand that power be held accountable to moral law — is one of Judaism's most enduring contributions to civilization.

**Diaspora:** After the destruction of the Second Temple by the Romans in 70 CE, Jews were scattered across the world. For 1,900 years, Judaism survived without a homeland, without a central temple, without political power — held together by the Torah, the Talmud, the synagogue, the family, and the sheer persistence of a people who refused to disappear. This is one of the most extraordinary experiments in civilizational survival in human history.

**The Messiah:** In traditional Judaism, the Messiah has not yet come. The Messiah will be a human leader — not divine — who will gather the Jewish people, rebuild the Temple, and establish an era of universal peace. This is one of the fundamental divergences from Christianity, which claims the Messiah has already come in Jesus.

### Part 2: Christianity — Incarnation and Its Consequences

Christianity makes the most audacious claim in the history of religion: that God became a human being.

**The Incarnation:** The doctrine that in Jesus of Nazareth, God entered human existence — was born as a baby, lived a human life, suffered, and died. This is not a metaphor in Christian theology. It is a literal claim about the nature of reality. If it is true, it changes everything. If it is false, it is — as the apostle Paul said — "of all men most pitiable."

**The Trinity:** One God in three persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Not three gods (that would be polytheism). Not one God in three modes (that would be modalism, an early heresy). One God who exists eternally as three persons in relationship. This is a metaphysical claim of considerable complexity, and it has produced two thousand years of philosophical debate.

**Original sin:** The doctrine that human nature is fundamentally broken by Adam's fall — that every human being is born with an inclination toward sin that cannot be overcome by mere effort. This produces Christian anthropology's deep skepticism about human perfectibility: humans cannot save themselves. They need grace — God's unearned gift.

**Grace and salvation:** The core Christian claim: humans are saved not by their own moral effort but by God's grace, made available through the death and resurrection of Jesus. The exact mechanics of this — how grace works, what role human choice plays, whether salvation can be lost — are the source of Christianity's most significant internal divisions.

**The church:** Christianity is not just a set of beliefs; it is a community — the church (ecclesia). The early church grew explosively across the Roman Empire. By 313 CE (the Edict of Milan), Christianity was legal. By 380 CE, it was the official religion of the Empire. This transformation from persecuted minority to state religion is one of history's most consequential shifts.

### Part 3: Denominational Diversity — Christianity's Internal Map

Christianity is not one thing. It is a family of traditions that agree on the Incarnation and the Resurrection but disagree about almost everything else:

**Roman Catholicism:** The oldest continuous institutional form. The Pope as the head of the church. The seven sacraments. The authority of tradition alongside scripture. The doctrine of transubstantiation (the bread and wine become the literal body and blood of Christ).

**Eastern Orthodoxy:** Split from Rome in 1054 (the Great Schism). Rich liturgical tradition. Icons as windows into the divine. The concept of theosis — the goal of human life is to become like God. Does not accept papal authority.

**Protestantism:** Born in the Reformation (1517, Martin Luther). Sola scriptura — scripture alone is the authority. Sola fide — salvation by faith alone. Sola gratia — by grace alone. Protestantism splintered into hundreds of denominations: Lutheran, Reformed, Baptist, Methodist, Anglican, Pentecostal, and many more. The splintering itself tells you something about the tradition — when you remove central authority, disagreements produce new churches.

### Part 4: What Judaism Produced

- **Ethical monotheism** — the idea that the one God of the universe cares about justice and mercy
- **The Hebrew Bible** — one of the most influential texts in human history
- **The Talmudic tradition** — argument as sacred practice; intellectual rigor as worship
- **The prophetic tradition** — the demand that power be accountable to moral law
- **Civilizational persistence** — survival without territory for 1,900 years
- **Disproportionate intellectual contribution** — Jews constitute about 0.2% of the world's population and have won approximately 22% of Nobel Prizes. This is not an accident. It is the product of a tradition that venerates learning.

### Part 5: What Christianity Produced

- **The institutional infrastructure of the West** — hospitals, universities, the preservation of classical learning in monasteries
- **Universal human dignity** — grounded in imago Dei (every human bears the image of God)
- **The abolition movement** — nearly every major abolitionist argued on explicitly Christian grounds
- **Western art, architecture, and music** — from Chartres Cathedral to Bach to Michelangelo
- **The concept of separating religious and political authority** — "Render unto Caesar what is Caesar's" (Jesus) laid the groundwork, though it took centuries to implement
- **Canon law as a precursor to modern legal systems**

### Part 6: The Honest Record — Judaism

**The covenant and chosenness:** Judaism claims that God chose the Jewish people for a specific covenant relationship. This has been criticized as tribal exclusivism. The honest response: Judaism extends the Noahide laws (seven moral laws) to all humanity; the concept of the righteous gentile recognizes non-Jews who live ethically; and the internal Jewish debate about what "chosenness" means — a privilege or a burden — is ongoing and sophisticated.

**The treatment of women in traditional Judaism:** Historical restrictions on women's participation in religious life — exclusion from public Torah reading, from the rabbinate in Orthodox traditions, from counting in a minyan. The internal reform — Conservative and Reform Judaism ordaining women, expanding roles — demonstrates that the tradition contains resources for self-correction.

### Part 7: The Honest Record — Christianity

**The Crusades (1096–1291):** Christian military campaigns to recapture Jerusalem. The First Crusade included the massacre of Jews in the Rhineland — Christian violence against Jews on the way to fight Muslims. The Fourth Crusade sacked Constantinople — Christians attacking Christians. The Crusades were theologically justified by the doctrine of holy war and by papal promises of spiritual reward. The reality was conquest, plunder, and enormous suffering.

**The Inquisition:** The Catholic Church's systematic investigation and punishment of heresy. The actual death toll (recent scholarship suggests several thousand over several centuries) was significantly lower than popular mythology, but the institution was genuinely repressive — it used torture, coerced confessions, and punished dissent. The theological logic: if heresy leads to eternal damnation, and the church has a duty to save souls, then compulsion is an act of love. This logic is internally coherent and morally horrifying. The student must understand both.

**Christian antisemitism:** For nearly two thousand years, Christians persecuted Jews — accusing them of deicide (killing God), restricting them to ghettos, expelling them from countries, inciting pogroms. The theological machinery of Christian antisemitism was formally repudiated only at Vatican II in 1965. The relationship between Christian antisemitism and the Holocaust is debated but cannot be avoided.

**The Wars of Religion (1524–1648):** A century of Christians killing Christians over which version of Christianity was correct. The Peace of Westphalia (1648) ended the worst of it by establishing the principle that the ruler determined the religion of their territory. This produced the modern state system — a political arrangement born from religious exhaustion.

### Part 8: The Relationship Between Judaism and Christianity

Christianity began as a Jewish sect. Jesus was a Jew. Paul was a Jew. The earliest Christians attended synagogues and observed Jewish law. The break came gradually — over the first two centuries CE — as Christianity increasingly defined itself in opposition to the Judaism from which it emerged.

The consequences were catastrophic for Jews. Christians read the Hebrew Bible as pointing to Christ — as the "Old Testament" superseded by the "New." This supersessionist theology — the claim that Christianity replaced Judaism as God's covenant people — provided the theological foundation for centuries of persecution.

The honest student holds both truths: Christianity owes its existence to Judaism, and Christianity treated Jews abominably for most of its history. Neither truth cancels the other.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Covenant — Mapping Judaism's Architecture [Early Packet]
Using the six diagnostic questions from 1.3.1, map Judaism as a worldview. What does Judaism say about what is ultimately real? How do Jews know truth? What does Judaism teach about human nature? Where does moral authority come from? What is life for?

### Study 2: The Incarnation — Mapping Christianity's Architecture [Early-Mid Packet]
Same exercise for Christianity. Where do the two maps agree? Where do they diverge? Identify the specific point of divergence: Jesus.

### Study 3: Reading Primary Texts [Ongoing]
Read the assigned biblical passages directly. After each reading: What is this passage saying? What does it assume about God? About humans? About right and wrong? How does a Jewish reader interpret this differently from a Christian reader?

### Study 4: The Talmudic Method [Mid-Packet]
Read selections from or about the Talmud. The student encounters the Talmudic style: an opinion, then a counter-opinion, then a third voice, then a resolution that preserves the dissent. Discuss: What does this method of argument tell you about what Judaism values? How is it different from traditions that produce a single authoritative answer?

### Study 5: Denominational Map [Mid-Packet]
Create a visual map of Christian denominations — Catholicism, Orthodoxy, and the major Protestant traditions. For each, identify what they believe about authority, salvation, and worship. What do they share? Where do they split?

### Study 6: The Honest Record [Late Packet]
Dedicated session on the Crusades, the Inquisition, and Christian antisemitism. Read age-appropriate accounts. Discuss: How did the people who did these things justify them? Were the justifications coherent? What does this teach us about the relationship between belief and behavior?

### Study 7: Steel-Manning Exercise [End of Packet]
The student picks whichever tradition is furthest from their own experience and presents its strongest case — as if advocating for it before someone who belongs to it. The mentor evaluates: Would a real adherent recognize this as fair?

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Worldview Mapping
**Developing well:** The student can map both traditions using the six diagnostic questions with accuracy and specificity.
**Developing slowly:** The student can list facts about each tradition but cannot organize them into a worldview framework.

### 2. Internal Logic
**Developing well:** The student can articulate the internal logic of each tradition — why a Jew follows 613 commandments, why a Christian believes in the Trinity — in terms the tradition's adherents would recognize.
**Developing slowly:** The student describes practices from the outside without understanding the theological reasoning.

### 3. Honest Record
**Developing well:** The student can present both achievements and failures honestly, with context, without either apologetics or prosecution.
**Developing slowly:** Either avoids the failures entirely or treats the failures as the whole story.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Christianity as the Default
If the student (or the mentor) is Christian, there is a natural tendency to present Christianity as the normative tradition and Judaism as the background. Resist this actively. The student should be able to present Judaism's strongest case with the same depth and conviction as Christianity's.

### Treating the Honest Record as Balance
The honest record sections are not "balance" — they are not included to make the traditions look bad to offset making them look good. They are included because they are true, because a student who does not know about them has been protected from history, and because understanding how sincere belief produces both good and harm is one of the most important lessons in 1.3.
